scope.area: plugin hook model, plugin discovery/loading, and bundled plugin readiness

summary.highLevelVerdict: Ready: bundled plugin discovery is repository-local, the default inventory matches the filesystem, the hook tree is centrally allocated, and the earlier SessionRouter hook-name drift has been reconciled in the shared hook config. Missing/problematic: some runtime consumers still do not use the hook model cleanly because parts of the engine flow emit wrapper hooks twice, QueueBroker points at a nonexistent MANAGER.QUEUE hook branch, logger-runtime listens to a hook that is not the live main heartbeat path, and override/unload semantics remain underspecified. Optional follow-up: clearer per-context activation rules and teardown lifecycles would make the plugin system easier to extend safely.

hookModelAndExecutor.question: Does the plugin system still provide a coherent hook model and a usable executor core?
hookModelAndExecutor.answer: Yes. Hook ids are still centrally allocated across SHARED, MAIN, ENGINE, MANAGER, and TENANT, listeners are registered with stable priority ordering, and callers that need strict failure behavior can use runWithContext(..., { rethrow: true }). This remains the strongest part of the plugin system.

evidence.note: Hook namespaces are centrally allocated across shared, main, engine, manager, and tenant contexts.
Listener registration records plugin ownership and sorts listeners by ascending priority.
run() and runWithContext() support mutable hook contexts, error forwarding, and optional rethrow.

itemsToGrantCorrectness.item: Hook ids are centrally allocated instead of being ad hoc
Listener priority ordering exists in the executor core
Critical callers can opt into hook-failure propagation

discoveryAndBundledInventory.question: Can the current runtime discover the bundled plugin set and match it against config coherently?
discoveryAndBundledInventory.answer: Yes. plugin-scan resolves the bundled plugin directory from the repository layout, scans an optional custom plugin directory afterward, and the default config enables only logger-runtime and error-reporter, which are the two bundled plugin files currently present.

evidence.note: Bundled plugins resolve from ../plugins and optional custom plugins are scanned afterward.
The default plugin inventory enables only logger-runtime and error-reporter.
logger-runtime exports a matching plugin identity.
error-reporter exports a matching plugin identity.

itemsToGrantCorrectness.item: The bundled plugin root can be resolved without an external alias definition
The default config only enables bundled plugins that currently exist
Bundled plugin config keys and exported plugin names align

hookConsumerConsistency.question: Do core runtime consumers use the shared hook tree consistently and once per lifecycle step?
hookConsumerConsistency.answer: Only partially. The previous SessionRouter mismatch is fixed because the MANAGER.SESSION tree now includes CREATE, CACHE_GET, and CACHE_SET, and SessionRouter uses those names directly. But there are still two important drifts: parts of the HTTP flow emit GET_ROUTER, GET_SESSION, and UPDATE_SESSION wrapper hooks both in the uWS handler and again inside Network2ManagerResolver, and QueueBroker still points to plugin.hooks.MANAGER.QUEUE even though the shared hook tree defines MANAGER.QUEUE_BROKER instead.

evidence.note: SessionRouter now uses GET_SESSION hooks from the MANAGER.SESSION tree.
SessionRouter also uses CREATE, CACHE_GET, and CACHE_SET hook groups that now exist in the shared hook config.
The MANAGER hook tree includes QUEUE_BROKER plus the expanded SESSION hook set.
The handler emits REQUEST.GET_ROUTER wrapper hooks around manager.resolveRoute().
The handler also emits REQUEST.GET_SESSION and RESPONSE.UPDATE_SESSION wrappers around manager calls.
Network2ManagerResolver emits GET_ROUTER wrapper hooks again inside the manager bridge.
Network2ManagerResolver also emits GET_SESSION hooks again.
Network2ManagerResolver emits RESPONSE.UPDATE_SESSION hooks again during session persistence.
QueueBroker still reads plugin.hooks.MANAGER.QUEUE even though the configured branch is QUEUE_BROKER.

itemsToGrantCorrectness.item: The current SessionRouter hook names exist in plugin-hooks.config
Core engine wrapper hooks are emitted exactly once per lifecycle step
QueueBroker points to an existing MANAGER hook namespace

contextLoadingAndBundledBehavior.question: Are plugins loaded into all runtime contexts in a useful and behaviorally coherent way?
contextLoadingAndBundledBehavior.answer: Only partially. MAIN, ENGINE, MANAGER, and TENANT all scan the plugin inventory, which keeps plugin boot behavior consistent across processes. But this is still mostly load-everywhere behavior without explicit per-context activation rules, and the bundled logger example remains weak because it listens to MAIN.PROCESS.HEARTBEAT while the live main heartbeat emission path runs through MAIN.SUPERVISOR.HEARTBEAT.

evidence.note: MAIN scans the configured plugin inventory during bootstrap.
ENGINE also scans the plugin inventory during bootstrap.
MANAGER also scans the same inventory.
TENANT app bootstrap also scans the shared plugin inventory.
logger-runtime registers against MAIN.PROCESS.HEARTBEAT.
The live main heartbeat emission path uses MAIN.SUPERVISOR.HEARTBEAT.

itemsToGrantCorrectness.item: All runtime contexts participate in plugin loading
The system has explicit per-context activation rules beyond generic loading and hook-range filtering
The bundled logger currently listens to a hook that is active in the live main heartbeat path

overrideAndUnloadSemantics.question: Are replacement, duplicate-name, and unload semantics fully defined today?
overrideAndUnloadSemantics.answer: Not yet. The loader supports both single-file and folder plugins and respects per-plugin enabled flags, but duplicate plugin names across bundled and custom directories simply overwrite the plugins Map entry after registration, without a defined override lifecycle. unload() also removes listeners and the map entry without giving the plugin a first-class teardown callback to release resources.

evidence.note: Duplicate plugin names are stored with plugins.set(pluginName, plugin) after registration, without explicit replacement policy.
unload() strips listeners and the plugin map entry, but there is no plugin teardown lifecycle.

itemsToGrantCorrectness.item: Plugins can be disabled by config key
There is a defined safe override policy for duplicate plugin names across directories
Plugins have a first-class teardown lifecycle during unload or replacement

mustFix: Stop double-emitting ENGINE GET_ROUTER, GET_SESSION, and UPDATE_SESSION wrapper hooks so plugin observers see one lifecycle event per real operation.
Align QueueBroker with plugin-hooks.config by using the MANAGER.QUEUE_BROKER hook branch instead of MANAGER.QUEUE.
shouldFix: Define override semantics for duplicate plugin names across bundled and custom directories.
Add a first-class teardown lifecycle for plugin unload or replacement.
Move from load-everywhere behavior toward clearer per-context activation rules.
niceToHave: Rework logger-runtime so the bundled examples observe hooks that are active in the real runtime path.
